4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Ask any AI company where their model’s training data came from, and watch the answer get vague fast.
That’s not evasion. It’s the honest state of the industry. Data provenance — the ability to say where a piece of training data originated, when it was collected, and whether it’s been altered since — is supposed to be table stakes for building anything responsibly. In practice, most of the AI supply chain can’t answer any of the three.
Three Questions, No Good Answers
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Provenance comes down to this:

• Where did it come from?

• When was it collected?

• Has it been modified since?
Most large models aren’t trained on a dataset. They’re trained on a dataset of datasets — sprawling composites stitched together from hundreds of upstream sources, re-hosted, re-packaged, and re-labeled so many times that the original trail goes cold. Attribution doesn’t survive the process. Often it was never recorded to begin with.
The Numbers Are Worse Than “We Don’t Know”
The Data Provenance Initiative, a multi-disciplinary research effort spanning legal and machine learning experts, put this to the test: it traced the lineage of more than 1,800 datasets widely used for fine-tuning. What it found wasn’t just a gap — it was a mess.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

More than 70% of licenses for popular datasets on hosting platforms like GitHub and Hugging Face turned out to be unspecified. And among the datasets that were labeled, the labels frequently lied: a re-annotation of licenses found that 66% were miscategorized, typically listed as more permissive than what the original creator actually intended.
Put plainly: most organizations fine-tuning on these datasets don’t know what they’re legally allowed to do with them — and they don’t know what’s actually inside them either. Not a compliance footnote. A structural blind spot at the base of the entire training pipeline.
Why This Isn’t Just a Legal Problem
Press enter or click to view image in full size

It’s tempting to file this under “lawyers will sort it out.” They won’t, not alone. Unknown provenance is also a quality and safety problem:

• You can’t audit what you can’t trace. If a model produces biased, toxic, or factually wrong output, tracing it back to a source dataset is nearly impossible when the chain of custody was never documented.

• You can’t filter what you can’t see. Contamination, duplication, and low-quality synthetic content all hide more easily in an unlabeled pile than in a documented one.

• You inherit risk you never agreed to. A dataset mislabeled as permissive can quietly expose a company to copyright and licensing liability it didn’t know it had taken on.
The Emerging Fix: Treat Data Like Software
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Software solved a version of this problem with the Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) — a standardized ingredient list showing exactly what components, licenses, and dependencies went into a piece of code. The AI field is starting to reach for the same idea: call it an ML-BOM, a documented, machine-readable record of what data went into a model, where it came from, and under what terms.
It’s a simple concept with an unglamorous name, and that’s exactly why it might work. Provenance doesn’t need to be reinvented — it needs to be tracked, consistently, the way every other engineered supply chain already tracks its inputs.
The Bottom Line
An industry that can tell you a model’s parameter count to the billion still can’t reliably tell you where its training data came from. That gap isn’t a rounding error — it’s the foundation the rest of the system sits on. Until provenance is treated as a first-class engineering requirement rather than an afterthought, “we don’t fully know what’s in there” will keep being the honest answer.
